ending: A Christmas Carol ends with Scrooge's complete redemption. After the three spirits have shown him his past, his present, and a bleak, lonely future ending at his own neglected grave, he wakes on Christmas morning overjoyed to find he still has time to change. He throws himself into making amends: he sends a large prize turkey to the Cratchit family, gives generously to the poor he had earlier scorned, and gladly joins his nephew Fred for Christmas dinner, mending the rift between them. The next morning he surprises Bob Cratchit with a raised wage and a promise to help his family. True to his word, Scrooge becomes a kind, generous, and beloved man, a second father to Tiny Tim, who recovers and lives. He keeps the goodwill of Christmas in his heart throughout the year and is remembered ever after as someone who knew how to keep Christmas well. The transformation is total and lasting: the bitter miser of the opening has become a source of warmth and charity to everyone around him.
in_short: On Christmas Eve in London, the cold-hearted miser Ebenezer Scrooge, who scorns the holiday and everyone's goodwill, is visited by the ghost of his dead partner, Jacob Marley. Weighed down by the chains of his own greed, Marley warns that Scrooge faces the same doomed afterlife unless he changes, and that three spirits will come to him. The Ghost of Christmas Past shows Scrooge his lonelier, gentler younger self and the love he lost to money; the Ghost of Christmas Present reveals the warmth of others' celebrations, including the poor but happy Cratchit family and their frail son Tiny Tim, whose life is in danger; and the silent Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come shows him an unmourned death that proves to be his own. Terrified and repentant, Scrooge wakes on Christmas morning transformed. He becomes generous and kind, helps the Cratchits and saves Tiny Tim, reconciles with his nephew, and keeps the spirit of Christmas for the rest of his life.

recaps.text: On a bitter Christmas Eve in London, the miserly Ebenezer Scrooge goes about his business with his usual contempt for the season. He rebuffs his warm-hearted nephew Fred's invitation to Christmas dinner, dismisses two men collecting charity for the poor, and grudgingly allows his cold, underpaid clerk Bob Cratchit the holiday off. Returning alone to his gloomy chambers, Scrooge is confronted by the ghost of his dead partner, Jacob Marley, who appears bound in a long, heavy chain he forged in life through his own greed and hard-heartedness. Marley warns Scrooge that he wears a similar chain, longer still, and that he faces the same fate of endless, remorseful wandering unless he changes. To give him a chance at escape, Marley tells Scrooge that he will be haunted by three spirits, then departs into the night, leaving the old man shaken but still doubtful.

The first of the promised visitors, the Ghost of Christmas Past, comes to Scrooge and carries him back into scenes from his own earlier life. He sees himself as a lonely, neglected schoolboy left behind during the holidays, and is moved by the memory. He revisits the warmth of his beloved younger sister and a joyful Christmas party thrown by Fezziwig, the generous employer who first taught him his trade and treated his workers with kindness. Most painfully, he watches the young woman he once loved release him from their engagement, recognizing that his growing obsession with wealth had crowded her out of his heart. These visions stir long-buried feelings, showing Scrooge the happier, softer man he had once been and the human warmth he has since abandoned in favor of money.

The Ghost of Christmas Present, a warm and jovial giant, shows Scrooge how the holiday is celebrated in the here and now. He is taken to the modest home of his clerk, Bob Cratchit, where a poor but loving family makes the most of a small Christmas dinner. There Scrooge notices Tiny Tim, Cratchit's frail, disabled youngest son, whose gentleness touches him; the spirit warns that unless things change, the boy will not survive. Scrooge also looks in on his nephew Fred's cheerful party, where the guests good-humoredly tease his miserliness even as Fred insists on wishing him well. Witnessing so much happiness among people who have far less than he does, Scrooge feels his hard indifference beginning to give way to concern for others, especially for the vulnerable child whose life now weighs on his mind.

The last spirit, the silent, hooded Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come, shows Scrooge grim glimpses of the future. He sees a man's death met with cold indifference, his possessions stolen and sold off, and no one moved to mourn him; acquaintances discuss the death casually, caring only whether there will be a free lunch at the funeral. He also sees the Cratchit household plunged into grief over the loss of Tiny Tim. Filled with dread, Scrooge begs to know the identity of the despised, unmourned dead man, and the phantom leads him to a neglected grave bearing his own name. Horrified to understand that this lonely, unlamented end is his own future, Scrooge pleads for a chance to change his ways, promising to honor Christmas and keep its lessons in his heart if only the future can still be altered.

Scrooge wakes on Christmas morning to find he is still alive and that the whole night's visits have left him a changed man, giddy with relief and joy. Determined to make amends, he sends a great prize turkey anonymously to the Cratchits, gives generously to the charity he had earlier scorned, and joyfully accepts his nephew Fred's invitation to Christmas dinner. The next day he startles Bob Cratchit by raising his wages and pledging to help his struggling family. From then on Scrooge becomes as kind and generous as he had once been cold, a second father to Tiny Tim, who does not die, and a man known for keeping Christmas well all through the year. The story closes with his complete transformation from bitter miser to warm-hearted benefactor.
